system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 16: Fundamental Harmonies of Scales

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 16 — Fundamental Harmonies of Scales

_Pages 84-87._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Triads in carol harmonization

> One of the many uses of triads is in harmonizing simple tunes like Christmas carols.

**Significance (p. 83).** Frames a major use-case for the triad chapter.

## 7th chord as triad: omit root or 5th

> When a 7th chord triad is desired, it is usually best to leave out the root or Sth.

**Significance (p. 83).** Voicing rule for reducing 7th chord to triad form.

## Relative minor on IV chord

> The technique of substituting the relative minor is often used on
> the IV chord of a key

**Significance (p. 84).** Specific application of relative-substitution at IV.

## V7 sets up new key

> the V7 (or sometimes V) chord almost always
> is the chord that “sets up” the new key; without some form of V7 or V chord, the transition to a new
> key sounds awkward.

**Significance (p. 84).** Modulation principle: V7 is required setup chord.

## Open voicing defined

> Open voicing refers to any arrangement of tones other than closed voicing.

**Significance (p. 84).** Defines open voicing as complement to closed.

## Open triad phrase-end bass-root rule

> WHEN
> USING OPEN TRIADS, THE ROOT IS USALLY PUT IN THE BASS FOR THE LAST CHORD IN
> A PHRASE

**Significance (p. 85).** Voicing rule for phrase endings using open triads.

## Doubling to make 4-note chord

> To make iiads into 4 note chords, you may double a root, 3rd, or 5th.

**Significance (p. 85).** Rule for promoting triad to 4-note voicing via doubling.

## Fundamental scale harmonies

> Every scale has fundamental harmonies or chords that can be built from it.

**Significance (p. 86).** Frames the harmonized-scale concept central to Section 16.

## Next-chord-up construction

> THE NEXT CHORD
> IS DERIVED BY MOVING EACH TONE IN THE IST CHORD UP TO THE NEXT TONE IN THE
> SCALE.

**Significance (p. 86).** Mechanical rule for constructing diatonic chord series.

## Bass-note-as-root naming

> The names of the chords are derived BY THINKING OF THE BASS NOTE OF EACH CHORD AS
> THE ROOT

**Significance (p. 86).** Naming rule for derived diatonic chords.

## vii° = V7 (no root)

> important point is that a VII diminished triad sounds like a dominant 7th chord whose root is 2
> whole steps lower.

**Significance (p. 86).** Identifies vii° as rootless V7 — a key substitution insight.

## Diatonic 4-note chord sequence

> You would then have the following chords: I+ m7 Im? IV# V7 VIm7 V. Im7>5 VIIIF.

**Significance (p. 86).** Catalogs the diatonic seventh-chord sequence.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
